questionText: For the quadratic equation $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$ with $a \neq 0$ and real coefficients, classify the condition $b^2 - 4ac \ge 0$ as necessary, sufficient, both, or neither, for the statement 'the equation has at least one real root'. Justify each direction.

steps.description: Set up the condition and the statement.
steps.workingLatex: P:\ \Delta = b^2 - 4ac \ge 0, \qquad Q:\ ax^2 + bx + c = 0 \text{ has a real root}
steps.explanation: We label the discriminant condition $P$ and the existence of a real root $Q$. Classifying $P$ means checking whether $P \Rightarrow Q$, whether $Q \Rightarrow P$, or both.

steps.description: Recall the meaning of necessary and sufficient.
steps.workingLatex: P \text{ sufficient} \Leftrightarrow P \Rightarrow Q; \quad P \text{ necessary} \Leftrightarrow Q \Rightarrow P
steps.explanation: A sufficient condition guarantees real roots; a necessary one is unavoidable if real roots exist. We will settle each label by proving the corresponding implication.

steps.description: Choose completing the square as the working tool.
steps.explanation: Because $a \neq 0$ we may divide through by $a$, which lets us complete the square. This single manipulation will expose both implications at once.
Rewriting the equation as a perfect square equal to a constant isolates $x$ inside a square. The constant on the right is built directly from the discriminant.

steps.description: Relate the sign of the right-hand side to the discriminant.
steps.explanation: The denominator $4a^2$ is strictly positive, so the right-hand side has the same sign as $\Delta$. Whether that side is non-negative is therefore controlled entirely by $\Delta$.

steps.description: State the key equivalence about real solutions.
steps.explanation: A real square is never negative, so the equation can be satisfied by a real $x$ only when the right-hand side is non-negative. This links the existence of a real root to the sign of $\Delta$.

steps.description: Prove the sufficient direction P implies Q.
steps.explanation: If $\Delta \ge 0$ then $\sqrt{\Delta}$ is real, so taking square roots produces genuine real values of $x$. Thus a non-negative discriminant guarantees at least one real root.

steps.description: Note the boundary and interior cases.
steps.explanation: When $\Delta=0$ the two roots coincide, still giving 'at least one' real root, and when $\Delta>0$ there are two. Either way $Q$ holds, so $P \Rightarrow Q$ is confirmed and $P$ is sufficient.

steps.description: Prove the necessary direction Q implies P.
steps.explanation: If a real root exists, substituting it makes the right-hand side equal to a real square, which is non-negative. Multiplying by the positive $4a^2$ forces $\Delta \ge 0$, so $Q \Rightarrow P$ and $P$ is necessary.

steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q \ \text{and}\ Q \Rightarrow P \ \Rightarrow \ P \Leftrightarrow Q
steps.explanation: Each statement implies the other, so a non-negative discriminant and the existence of a real root are logically equivalent. The two-way arrow captures this.

steps.workingLatex: b^2 - 4ac \ge 0 \text{ is necessary and sufficient for a real root}
steps.explanation: Equivalence means the discriminant condition is both necessary and sufficient. So $\Delta \ge 0$ is exactly the condition for the quadratic to have at least one real root.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $P \Leftrightarrow Q$: $b^2 - 4ac \ge 0$ is both necessary and sufficient for $ax^2+bx+c=0$ (with $a \neq 0$) to have at least one real root.
